content.text: An ARM template deployment is the act of submitting a JSON or Bicep template to ARM, which then validates, plans, and executes the resource operations described in the template. The deployment creates a permanent record in deployment history and returns a provisioning state (Succeeded, Failed, or Canceled).

Templates can be sourced from a local file, a URI to a blob storage object, a Template Spec stored in Azure, or an inline JSON string passed directly on the command line. All paths lead to the same ARM processing engine.
An ARM JSON template has a fixed top-level schema with six optional sections. Only resources is required; the others provide metadata, input flexibility, and reusable values.
Identifies the template schema and controls which functions and features are available
User-defined version string; ARM does not validate or enforce this
Input values supplied at deployment time; support type constraints, defaults, and allowed-values
Intermediate values computed once from parameters/functions and reused throughout resources
The resource declarations ARM will create, update, or manage
Values returned after deployment; accessible from the CLI, SDK, and parent templates

# Deploy from a local Bicep file with a parameter file
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @prod.params.json \
  --parameters environment=prod \
  --name deploy-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M)

# Deploy from a storage URI (template must be publicly readable or use SAS)
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-uri 'https://myacct.blob.core.windows.net/templates/main.json?sv=...&sig=...' \
  --parameters environment=staging

# Read deployment outputs after completion
az deployment group show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name deploy-20240301-1200 \
  --query properties.outputs

# Deploy from local file; splatting keeps the command readable
$params = @{
  ResourceGroupName       = 'myRG'
  TemplateFile            = './main.bicep'
  TemplateParameterFile   = './prod.params.json'
  Name                    = "deploy-$(Get-Date -Format yyyyMMdd-HHmm)"
}
New-AzResourceGroupDeployment @params

# Show outputs
(Get-AzResourceGroupDeployment -ResourceGroupName 'myRG' -Name 'deploy-20240301-1200').Outputs

A parameter file is a JSON document that supplies values for template parameters. Separating parameters from the template lets you maintain multiple environment configurations (dev, staging, prod) without duplicating template logic.

The reference key replaces the value key for secrets: ARM fetches the secret from Key Vault at deployment time and injects it into the parameter without exposing it in the parameter file, deployment history, or CLI output.

A Template Spec is an ARM resource type (Microsoft.Resources/templateSpecs) that stores a versioned ARM template in Azure. Instead of distributing template files, teams publish Template Specs and consumers deploy directly from the spec by ID, ensuring everyone uses the approved version.

# Publish a template as a Template Spec
az ts create \
  --name storage-template \
  --version '1.0.0' \
  --resource-group shared-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file storage.bicep \
  --description 'Standard storage account template'

# Get the Template Spec resource ID
SPEC_ID=$(az ts show \
  --name storage-template \
  --version '1.0.0' \
  --resource-group shared-rg \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Deploy from the Template Spec
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-spec "$SPEC_ID" \
  --parameters storageName=myacctprod

Outputs defined in a template are accessible after deployment via the CLI, SDK, and as inputs to parent templates in nested deployments. They are the primary mechanism for passing resource IDs or endpoints between deployment stages.

# Read a specific output value
az deployment group show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name my-deploy \
  --query 'properties.outputs.storageId.value' \
  -o tsv

# Capture storage ID into a shell variable for use in subsequent commands
STORAGE_ID=$(az deployment group show \
  --resource-group myRG --name my-deploy \
  --query 'properties.outputs.storageId.value' -o tsv)
echo "Deployed storage: $STORAGE_ID"

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

deployment = client.deployments.get(
    resource_group_name='myRG',
    deployment_name='my-deploy',
)

outputs = deployment.properties.outputs
if outputs:
    storage_id = outputs.get('storageId', {}).get('value')
    print(f'Storage resource ID: {storage_id}')

ARM validates a template before executing any resource operations. The az deployment group validate command runs the full server-side validation — schema check, parameter type enforcement, resource provider availability, and quota checks — without deploying anything.

# Validate without deploying
az deployment group validate \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @prod.params.json

# Validation errors appear in the 'error' field of the JSON output
az deployment group validate \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --query 'error' -o json

az deployment group validate does not check for resource naming conflicts or quota availability in all cases. Use --what-if for a more accurate pre-flight check that also shows which resources will change.

Large deployments are commonly split across multiple template files. ARM supports two composition patterns: nested templates (template body inline inside the parent) and linked templates (separate files referenced by URI or Template Spec ID). Bicep modules compile to linked templates automatically.

# Upload templates to blob storage and generate SAS tokens
ACCOUNT=mytemplatesstorage
CONTAINER=templates
EXPIRY=$(date -u -d '1 hour' '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ')

az storage blob upload \
  --account-name $ACCOUNT --container-name $CONTAINER \
  --name storage.json --file storage.json

SAS=$(az storage blob generate-sas \
  --account-name $ACCOUNT --container-name $CONTAINER \
  --name storage.json --permissions r --expiry $EXPIRY -o tsv)

BLOB_URI="https://${ACCOUNT}.blob.core.windows.net/${CONTAINER}/storage.json?${SAS}"

# Pass the URI as a parameter to the parent template
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-file main.json \
  --parameters storageTemplateUri="$BLOB_URI"
Bicep compiles modules to linked templates when deploying from a local directory. Use az bicep build to inspect the generated ARM JSON and see how Bicep resolves module references.
ARM templates include a library of built-in functions for string manipulation, resource references, conditional logic, and math. These are evaluated server-side at deployment time.
add, sub, mul, div, mod, min, max

ARM evaluates the dependency graph in the template before deploying resources. Dependencies can be declared explicitly with dependsOn or inferred implicitly when one resource uses the reference() function to read a property of another.

In the example above, myNIC has an explicit dependsOn on myVNet. The implicit dependency via reference('myVNet') would also work, but explicit dependsOn is clearer for resources whose properties do not reference each other directly.
The copy element deploys multiple instances of a resource or property from a single declaration. It is the ARM JSON equivalent of Bicep's for expression.

Setting mode to Serial instead of Parallel deploys one instance at a time. The batchSize property controls how many instances deploy concurrently in serial mode — useful when later instances depend on earlier ones completing first.
Run az deployment group validate to get the exact field path
dependsOn missing; parent resource not yet created
Add explicit dependsOn or use reference() to create implicit dep
Use uniqueString() or a prefix that includes a unique suffix
Storage name >24 chars or contains uppercase/special chars
Use toLower(substring(...)) to normalize the name
Subscription vCPU or resource count limit reached
Request quota increase or reduce instance count
Reference in parameter file targets wrong vault or secret name
Verify vault ID and secretName; check SP has Get permission on vault

The Python SDK provides fine-grained control over the deployment lifecycle: submit, poll, retrieve outputs, and handle errors programmatically. This is useful for CI/CD tooling that needs to react to deployment results.

import json, time
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient
from azure.mgmt.resource.resources.v2022_09_01.models import (
    Deployment, DeploymentProperties, DeploymentMode
)

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

# Load template and parameters from disk
with open('main.json') as f:
    template = json.load(f)
with open('prod.params.json') as f:
    raw_params = json.load(f)

params = {k: {'value': v['value']} for k, v in raw_params['parameters'].items()}

deployment_name = f'deploy-{int(time.time())}'

poller = client.deployments.begin_create_or_update(
    resource_group_name=rg_name,
    deployment_name=deployment_name,
    parameters=Deployment(
        properties=DeploymentProperties(
            mode=DeploymentMode.INCREMENTAL,
            template=template,
            parameters=params,
        )
    ),
)

print(f'Deployment submitted: {deployment_name}')

# Poll until complete (LROPoller handles retry internally)
result = poller.result()  # blocks until Succeeded or raises on Failed

state = result.properties.provisioning_state
print(f'Final state: {state}')

# Extract outputs
if result.properties.outputs:
    for key, output in result.properties.outputs.items():
        print(f'  {key} = {output["value"]}')
Bicep vs ARM JSON Template Comparison

Bicep is the recommended authoring language for ARM templates. It compiles to ARM JSON at deployment time, meaning every Bicep template has a 1:1 equivalent ARM JSON template. The key differences are in authoring ergonomics.

The equivalent ARM JSON template would require approximately 3x more lines. Bicep's ternary operator, string interpolation, and module system all compile to valid ARM JSON that ARM's deployment engine processes identically.
Use az bicep build --file main.bicep to inspect the compiled ARM JSON. This is useful for auditing, understanding what ARM will receive, or integrating with tools that only accept ARM JSON.

ARM template deployments accept templates from four sources (local file, URI, Template Spec, or inline JSON) and process them through a validation-and-execution pipeline. Parameter files separate environment-specific values from template logic; Key Vault references keep secrets out of files and deployment history. Template Specs provide versioned, centrally managed templates consumable by any team. Deployment outputs pass resource IDs and endpoints to downstream consumers. Always validate with az deployment group validate or --what-if before running production deployments.
